We will see in this unit our third computational model, the WHILE language. The WHILE language can be seen as a simplified programming language, as it only uses natural numbers, and has only four different assignment statements and a single iterative instruction. This model will permit us to analyze features like the function computed by a program and its the temporal complexity. UNIT 10 The WHILE language 10.1 Introduction AJ Kfoury, MA Arbib, RN Moll, A programming approach to computability, Springer Verlag, 1982 The four assignment statements for setting the values of variables are: -- Assign a variable the zero constant value : X i := 0 -- Assign a variable the value of other variable: X i := X j -- Assign a variable the value of other variable incremented by one unit: X i := X j + 1 -- Assign a variable the value of other variable decremented by one unit: X i := X j вҖ“ 1 In a program written in the language WHILE (i.e., a WHILE program) the variable identifiers have the form: X 1 , X 2 , X 3 , ... , X i , ... a WHILE program) the variable identifiers have the form: X 1 , X 2 , X 3 , ... , X i , ... The iterative control instruction is an indefinite loop (a вҖңwhileвҖқ instruction), that has also a very simple control condition: checking whether the value of a given variable (the control variable) is not equal to zero. The control instruction is an indefinite loop of the form : while X i пӮ№ 0 do (the header of the loop) code (the body of the loop) od (the tail or end of the loop) A code is a finite non-empty sequence of statements separated by the symbol вҖң;вҖқ. Note: The iterative control sentence is refereed as "loop", "while loop" or simply "while" sentence. We say that a program is something more than a code as a WHILE program is a triple (n, p, s), where n and p are naturals with p пӮі n and p > 0 ; and s is a code following the previous sintaxis. a WHILE program is a triple (n, p, s) then the code has p variables in use: ( X 1 , X 2 , X 3 , ... , X p ) that can take natural values. n out of the p variables are input variables ( X 1 , X 2 , X 3 , ... , X n ), and the rest are denoted auxiliar variables. The (p вҖ“ n) auxiliar variables are implicitly set to zero at the beggining of the execution of the program. There is no input-output instructions, as the output of the program is the value of variable X 1 when the code finishes. The functioning of a WHILE program consists (as it is a model based on a structural language) in executing sequencially the instructions that compose the code, starting from the first one. The rest of the statements are executed once at a time when the previous one has fnished. The decremental assignment statement ( X i := X j вҖ“ 1 ) is executed in a way such that the value of the variable is zero in case that the decremental variable value was zero before the execution. In relationship to the iterative control instruction (while sentence), this first check whether the control variable (the one in the header) is different from zero; and in this case executes the code in the body of the loop, checking again the value of the control variable after this. This procedure is repeated while the checked variable is not equal to zero, as in one of these checks the value of the control variable is zero the while loop finishes. UNIT 4 Finite Automata We will see in this unit a restricted model of computation called a Finite Automaton, to see later on more sophisticated devices for accepting and generating languages. Finite Automata (FA) are no other than a different way of representing regular languages, in particular they are regular language recognition devices. FA shares with a computer the fact that they have вҖңcentral processing unitвҖқ of fixed, finite capacity. It receives its input as a string and delivered no output except an indication of whether or not the input is considered acceptable. What makes a FA a restricted system is the absence of memory outside its fixed central processor. We will see first, deterministic FA, and then the non-deterministic ones, to see later an equivalence between them. 1 2 4.1 Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA) A deterministic finite automaton (DFA) is a mathematical model of a discrete system comprising a finite set of possible internal states, and where the actual internal state of the system is the only information about previous inputs. Then, the behaviour of a DFA will be determined, exclusively, by its actual state and the future inputs. Let МҒs see before given a formal definition of a

previous inputs. Then, the behaviour of a DFA will be determined, exclusively, by its actual state and the future inputs. Let МҒs see before given a formal definition of a DFA , its structure and functioning. A DFA is a language recognizing device, in particular, of regular languages. The input string to be (or not) recognized is given by an input tape, divided in blocks containing a symbol in each block. Think a DFA as a ideal machine, according to the following description: The finite control is a "black box" that can be in a single state from a finite set of possible ones. Each state is labelled as final or not final, and there is always one state corresponding to the initial state (usually q 0 ) in which the finite control is at the begining. The reading head is controlled by the finite control and send to it the content of the reading block. The head can only move one block to the right and starts reading from the left of the input tape. The finite control, depending of the read symbol and of the present state, changes its state and moves the reading head. When the end

the left of the input tape. The finite control, depending of the read symbol and of the present state, changes its state and moves the reading head. When the end of the tape is reached, the DFA stops. If the automaton finishes in a state belonging to the set of вҖңfinal statesвҖқ then the string is accepted. The accepted language by the DFA is the set of accepted strings. 3 Definition 4.1: Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA) A deterministic finite automata (DFA) is a quintuple M = ( K , пҒ“ , пҒӨ , s , F ) where: K is a finite non empty set of states; пҒ“ is an alphabet; s is the initial state (s пғҺ K ) ; F is the set of final states (F пғҚ K ) ; пҒӨ is a transition function defined as пҒӨ: K пӮҙ пҒ“ вҶ’ K . The introduction of non-determinism gives new possibilities for the automata, for example for a given input and state, several next states can be possible (even none). Also it is possible to read more than one symbol in a single step, and evenmore to transit between states reading the empty string. Non-determinism permits that for a single string there will be several computations, and that for some of them the automata can be blocked (there is no next possible configurations even if there is still symbols of the string to be read). Blocked computation are considered non accepted computations. A string is accepted by a NDFA if presenting the string as input there is a computation that accepts it. PART II -- Theory of Computation Unit 8: The Turing Machine 8.1 Introduction 8.2 Formal definition 8.3 Examples The Turing Machine Introduced in 1936 by the English mathematician Alan Turing, the Turing machine is an ideal machine that seems capable of computing every function that can be computed (Church-Turing Thesis). Any efforts to augment its computing capabilities have failed and then we can see the Turing machines as a stable and maximal class of computational devices. In essence , a Turing Machine (TM) consists of a finite-state control unit and an infinite tape. TM вҖў вҖў вҖўвҖў вҖў вҖў The tape is unidimensional and left and right directions are clearly defined. Every cell contains a symbol of the TM alphabet or a special symbol (*) to indicate that the cell is empty. The TM has a single Read/Write head, that in a given moment see only one cell, the scanned cell of the tape. infinite tape Finite-state control head The number of occupied cells on the tape is finite, and the whole content of the tape is denoted as tape expression. The control unit operates in discrete steps; at each step it performs an elemental operation according its current state

the whole content of the tape is denoted as tape expression. The control unit operates in discrete steps; at each step it performs an elemental operation according its current state and the tape symbol currently scanned by the head. A TM can execute one of the following elemental operations: вҖў Write a symbol of the alphabet on the actual cell. вҖў Move the head one cell to the right. вҖў Move the head one cell to the left. вҖў Halt (stop). It also decides what the following state of the control unit will be. At any given time a TM is in one of several possible states, except at the beginning of the operation when the TM is in the initial state. Thus, before a computing step, that always occurrs except when the elemental instruction is to halt, the whole information material is: - the tape expression - the current scanned cell and on the TM: - current state. After the computing step we have: - a new tape expression (it can be the same one of the previous step) - a new scanned cell (it can be the same of the previous step) and, on the TM: a new

(it can be the same one of the previous step) - a new scanned cell (it can be the same of the previous step) and, on the TM: a new current state (it can be the same of the previous step). A Turing Machine (TM) is a quintuple M = ( K , q 0 , ОЈ , Оі , Оҙ ) where: K is a non-empty finite set of states ; q 0 is the initial state ( q 0 вҲҲ K ); ОЈ is an alphabet (a non-empty finite set of symbols); Оі is an instruction function defined as: Оі : K Г— ОЈ вҲӘ { a 0 } вҶ’ ОЈ МҒ ; Оҙ is a transition function defined as Оҙ : K Г— ОЈ вҲӘ { a 0 } вҶ’ K . Recursive and recursively enumerable languages A recursively enumerable language is a formal language for which there exists a Turing machine (or other computable function) that will halt and accept when presented with any string in the language as input but may either halt and reject or loop forever when presented with a string not in the language. Contrast this to recursive languages, which require that the Turing machine halts in all cases. Type-3 languages are recursive sets, they can be decided by FDA. Type-2 languages are recursive sets, they can be decided by NDPA. Type-1 languages are recursive sets, they can be decided by linear bounded TMs. Type-0 languages are recursively enumerable sets, they can only be semi-decided by TMs.

-- Initial (basic) Functions -- Combining functions: Operators -- Definition of the set of пҒӯ-Recursive Functions -- Examples -- Computability and further considerations UNIT 9 пҒӯ-Recursive Functions 9.1 Introduction We have seen in the previous unit the Turing Machine model to then formalize the concept of computable function. In this unit, we will analyze the concept of computable function in a constructive way, and the model will be implicitly defined. The idea behind "пҒӯ-recursive Functions" is quite simple: choose some very simple functions (initial functions) that we can consider to be trivially computable. Then define three operations over the functions (composition, primitive recursion and minimalization). We will see that a function is a пҒӯ-recursive function if it is an initial function or if it can be generated from the initial functions through the application (a finite number of times) of the three mentioned operations. In this way, the set of all пҒӯ-recursive functions (denoted as пҒӯ-REC) is the minimum set that contains the initial functions and that is closed for the operations of composition, primitive recursion and minimalization. Note: Do not get confused by the set of пҒӯ-recursive functions and the set of functions called primitive recursives (usually denoted as

the operations of composition, primitive recursion and minimalization. Note: Do not get confused by the set of пҒӯ-recursive functions and the set of functions called primitive recursives (usually denoted as PRIM). The set PRIM is a proper subset of пҒӯ-REC, that is, all primitive recursive functions are пҒӯ-recursive functions but the converse is not true. Their definition is similar to пҒӯ-REC but the minimalization operator is not included, and thus all functions are total functions.
